good_beginner_design_habits.label: Good Beginner Design Habits
good_beginner_design_habits.summary: Learn how to keep tables focused, separate repeated information, use IDs consistently, and design tables in ways that make future queries easier.

sketch0.title: How do you keep tables focused?

sketch1.title: How do you separate repeated information?

sketch2.title: How do you use IDs consistently?

sketch3.title: How do you design for future queries?

keep_tables_focused.title: Focused tables are easier to understand and easier to relate cleanly.
keep_tables_focused.bodyMarkdown:
A beginner table works best when it has one clear job.

For example:

- `students` stores student rows
- `courses` stores course rows
- `orders` stores order rows

That makes the design easier to read and extend later.

So the big idea is:

> each table should have one clear purpose

separate_repeated_information.title: Repeated information should often be moved into its own table.
separate_repeated_information.bodyMarkdown:
If the same information appears again and again, that is often a design clue.

For example, instead of storing the full department name inside every student row, a design can use:

- `departments`
- and a `department_id` in `students`

So the key idea is:

> repeated information often belongs in its own table

use_ids_consistently.title: Using ids consistently makes relationships easier to build and read.
use_ids_consistently.bodyMarkdown:
A common beginner habit is to use id-based links consistently.

That means patterns like:

- `students.id`
- `courses.id`
- `enrollments.student_id`
- `enrollments.course_id`

So the main idea is:

> consistent id naming makes relational design clearer

design_for_future_queries.title: A clean design makes future SELECT, JOIN, and reporting queries easier.
design_for_future_queries.bodyMarkdown:
A table design is not only for storage.
It is also for future questions.

If the tables are clear and linked well, later queries become easier to write and understand.

That is also why it helps to inspect designs in:

- **Tables** for structure
- **ERD** for relationship shape
- **Chen** for entity thinking

So the important idea is:

> good design today makes future queries easier tomorrow

m14_s03_create_focused_tables.title: Keep tables focused
m14_s03_create_focused_tables.prompt:
The SQLite workspace is using the blank `ddl_blank` dataset.

Write SQL that creates these two focused tables:

1. `students`
- `id` as `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`
- `name` as `TEXT NOT NULL`

2. `student_contacts`
- `id` as `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`
- `student_id` as `INTEGER NOT NULL`
- `email` as `TEXT`
- `phone_number` as `TEXT`
- `FOREIGN KEY (student_id)` references `students(id)`

Then click **Run**.

help.concept: Focused design keeps the main student record separate from extra contact details.
help.hint_2: The design habit here is not only the foreign key. It is the decision to let each table have one clearer job.

m14_s03_create_focused_tables.hint: Separate the main student row from the extra contact details.
m14_s03_create_focused_tables.starterCode: -- Create focused students and student_contacts tables

m14_s03_separate_repeated_information.title: Separate repeated information
m14_s03_separate_repeated_information.prompt:
The dataset is blank.

Write SQL that creates these two tables:

1. `departments`
- `id` as `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`
- `name` as `TEXT NOT NULL`

2. `students`
- `id` as `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`
- `department_id` as `INTEGER NOT NULL`
- `name` as `TEXT NOT NULL`
- `FOREIGN KEY (department_id)` references `departments(id)`

Then run it and inspect **ERD**.

help.concept: Separating repeated department information into its own table helps avoid repeating the same department name in every student row.
help.hint_2: The main design habit is moving the repeated department information into its own table and using a department id to refer to it.

m14_s03_separate_repeated_information.hint: Move the repeated department information into its own table.
m14_s03_separate_repeated_information.starterCode: -- Create departments and students with a clean link

m14_s03_use_ids_consistently.title: Use IDs consistently
m14_s03_use_ids_consistently.prompt:
The dataset is blank.

Write SQL that creates these three tables using consistent id naming:

1. `categories`
- `id` as `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`
- `name` as `TEXT NOT NULL`

2. `products`
- `id` as `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`
- `category_id` as `INTEGER NOT NULL`
- `name` as `TEXT NOT NULL`
- `FOREIGN KEY (category_id)` references `categories(id)`

3. `reviews`
- `id` as `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`
- `product_id` as `INTEGER NOT NULL`
- `rating` as `INTEGER NOT NULL`
- `FOREIGN KEY (product_id)` references `products(id)`

Then click **Run**.

help.concept: Consistent id naming helps learners read parent-child links more confidently.
help.hint_1: Use `id` in the parent tables and `category_id` or `product_id` in the child tables.
help.hint_2: The design habit here is consistency: each child link clearly shows what parent id it points to.

m14_s03_use_ids_consistently.hint: Use parent ids and matching child foreign-key names consistently.
m14_s03_use_ids_consistently.starterCode: -- Create three tables using consistent id naming

m14_s03_design_for_future_queries.title: Design for future queries
m14_s03_design_for_future_queries.prompt:
The dataset is blank.

Write SQL that creates these three tables:

1. `students`
- `id` as `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`
- `name` as `TEXT NOT NULL`

2. `courses`
- `id` as `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`
- `title` as `TEXT NOT NULL`

3. `enrollments`
- `id` as `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`
- `student_id` as `INTEGER NOT NULL`
- `course_id` as `INTEGER NOT NULL`
- `FOREIGN KEY (student_id)` references `students(id)`
- `FOREIGN KEY (course_id)` references `courses(id)`

Then click **Run**.
After that, inspect **Tables**, **ERD**, and **Chen** and think about how this design would help later join queries.

help.concept: A clean, linked design makes future queries easier because the relationships are explicit and the tables are focused.
help.hint_1: Create the parent tables first, then the bridge table.
help.hint_2: The many-to-many structure here is not only a relationship lesson. It is also a future-query lesson because it gives later joins a clean path.

m14_s03_design_for_future_queries.hint: Create the parent tables first, then the clean bridge table.
m14_s03_design_for_future_queries.starterCode: -- Create a design that will be easy to query later

m14_s03_design_habits_project.title: Practice good beginner design habits
m14_s03_design_habits_project.prompt:
Use the SQL workspace on the right to practice good beginner design habits on the blank `ddl_blank` dataset.

For each task:

1. write the CREATE TABLE statements carefully
2. click **Run**
3. inspect the created structure in **Tables**
4. inspect **ERD** and **Chen** when foreign keys are present

Tasks:

1. Create focused `students` and `student_contacts` tables
2. Create `departments` and `students` to separate repeated information
3. Create `categories`, `products`, and `reviews` using consistent ids
4. Create `students`, `courses`, and `enrollments` for future joins
5. Create `authors` and `books` with a clean parent-child link

Run one query at a time.
This project is about building design habits that make future SQL easier.

help.concept: Good beginner design habits are not random rules. They are habits that make the database clearer now and easier to query later.
help.hint_1: Keep asking whether a table has one clear purpose and whether repeated information should be separated.
help.hint_2: Solve the tasks one by one. Focus on table purpose first, then relationship clarity, then whether the schema will make future queries easier.

m14_s03_design_habits_project.hint: Ask whether each table has one clear job and whether the ids and links are easy to follow.
m14_s03_design_habits_project.starterCode:
-- Task 1
-- Create focused students and student_contacts tables

m14_s03_focused_tables_meaning.title: Why keep tables focused?
m14_s03_focused_tables_meaning.prompt: Why is it helpful to keep tables focused in beginner design?

help.concept: Focused tables are easier to understand and easier to link cleanly.
help.hint_1: Think one clear job per table.
help.hint_2: When a table has a clear purpose, the whole design becomes easier to reason about.

m14_s03_focused_tables_meaning.hint: Think one clear purpose per table.

options.a: Because focused tables are easier to understand and link cleanly
options.b: Because every table should try to store everything
options.d: Because focused tables cannot be queried later

m14_s03_repeated_info_meaning.title: Why separate repeated information?
m14_s03_repeated_info_meaning.prompt: Why is separating repeated information a good design habit?

help.concept: Repeated information is often a clue that the design can be clearer and less repetitive with a separate table.
help.hint_1: Think repeated department or category names.
help.hint_2: A separate table plus an id reference can make the design cleaner.

m14_s03_repeated_info_meaning.hint: Think repeated information often belongs in its own table.

options.a: Because repeated information often belongs in its own table
options.b: Because repetition always makes a design clearer
options.c: Because ids should never be reused as links
options.d: Because foreign keys remove all column names

m14_s03_ids_consistently_meaning.title: Why use IDs consistently?
m14_s03_ids_consistently_meaning.prompt: Why is consistent id naming helpful in relational design?

help.concept: Consistent id naming makes the parent-child links easier to read and follow.
help.hint_2: The naming pattern helps the reader see what each foreign key points to.

m14_s03_ids_consistently_meaning.hint: Think consistent names make the links easier to follow.

options.a: Because consistent id naming makes relationships easier to read
options.b: Because ids should always be random words
options.c: Because consistent ids remove foreign keys
options.d: Because id naming has no effect on clarity

m14_s03_future_queries_meaning.title: What does it mean to design for future queries?
m14_s03_future_queries_meaning.prompt: What is the main idea of designing tables for future queries?

help.concept: A clean design now makes later SELECT and JOIN queries easier to write and understand.
help.hint_1: Think better schema, easier future SQL.
help.hint_2: Good design helps the future reader, not just the current CREATE TABLE statement.

m14_s03_future_queries_meaning.hint: Think good design now makes later queries easier.

options.a: A cleaner design now makes later queries easier
options.b: Future queries do not matter when designing tables
options.c: Table design should ignore how the data will be queried
options.d: Future queries only matter after DELETE

m14_s03_tools_usage_meaning.title: How can the learner use the result-pane tools here?
m14_s03_tools_usage_meaning.prompt: How should a learner use the SQL result-pane tools in this section?

help.concept: The learner should inspect the created structure first, then inspect the relationships once they exist.
help.hint_1: Think Tables first, then ERD and Chen when links appear.
help.hint_2: Each tab helps with a different part of understanding the design.

m14_s03_tools_usage_meaning.hint: Think structure first, then relationship diagrams.

options.a: Use Tables to inspect created columns
options.b: Use ERD once foreign keys create relationships
options.c: Use Chen to see entities and attributes more conceptually
options.d: Ignore the views after running CREATE TABLE
options.e: Compare what each tab makes easier to notice

m14_s03_habits_true.title: What is true about good beginner design habits?
m14_s03_habits_true.prompt: Select all true statements about good beginner relational design habits.

help.concept: Good habits make the schema clearer, less repetitive, and easier to query later.
help.hint_1: Think focused tables, less repetition, clear ids.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about clarity and future usefulness.

m14_s03_habits_true.hint: Think clarity, less duplication, easier future queries.

options.a: Keeping tables focused can improve clarity
options.b: Separating repeated information can reduce duplication
options.c: Consistent ids can make links easier to read
options.d: Design habits do not affect future queries
options.e: Good design habits support future SQL work

m14_s03_focus_repeat_true.title: What is true about focus and repetition?
m14_s03_focus_repeat_true.prompt: Select all true statements about keeping tables focused and separating repeated information.

help.concept: A clearer design often comes from giving tables focused roles and moving repeated information into its own table.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about cleaner structure and less repetition.

m14_s03_focus_repeat_true.hint: Think focused tables and less repeated information.

options.a: A table with one clear job is often easier to understand
options.b: Repeated information can be a design clue
options.c: A separate table can sometimes store the repeated information more cleanly
options.d: Repeated information should always stay copied in every table
options.e: These habits can support relational clarity

m14_s03_ids_queries_true.title: What is true about IDs and future queries?
m14_s03_ids_queries_true.prompt: Select all true statements about consistent ids and designing for future queries.

help.concept: Consistent id links help both schema reading and later SQL writing.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about clear links and future query ease.

m14_s03_ids_queries_true.hint: Think clearer ids now, easier queries later.

options.a: Consistent ids can make relationships easier to follow
options.b: A clear linked schema can make future joins easier
options.c: Bridge tables can support future many-to-many queries
options.d: Future query thinking should never affect design
options.e: Schema clarity helps both people and SQL

m14_s03_design_habits_workflow_true.title: What matches a good beginner workflow for design habits?
m14_s03_design_habits_workflow_true.prompt: Select all statements that match a good beginner workflow for applying good relational design habits.

help.concept: A good workflow is to define each table's job clearly, separate repeated information, use ids consistently, and inspect the resulting design.
help.hint_2: Choose the steps that help produce a cleaner schema from the start.

m14_s03_design_habits_workflow_true.hint: Define the table purpose first, then separate repetition and use ids consistently.

options.b: Look for repeated information that may deserve its own table
options.c: Use ids consistently across parent and child tables
options.d: Inspect the design after creating it
options.e: Ignore whether the schema will be easy to query later
